theory.question: When does a single-node database stop being enough and you need sharding — with concrete capacity numbers for PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis, and DynamoDB
theory.answer:
Shard when data volume or write throughput exceeds what one node can store or process; if reads are the bottleneck, replication alone may suffice.

"The primary reason for sharding a database is scalability. Sharding is a solution if the volume of data or the write throughput has become too great for a single node to handle." Kleppmann adds: "(If read throughput is the problem, you don't necessarily need sharding—you can use read scaling, as discussed in Chapter 6.)" And: "If your data volume and write throughput are such that a single machine can handle them (and a single machine can do a lot nowadays!), it's often better to avoid sharding and stick with a single-shard database."

PostgreSQL (single node, no external sharding layer):
- Storage ceiling on managed Postgres is large but finite — AWS RDS PostgreSQL supports up to 64 TiB per instance; io2 storage can reach 256,000 provisioned IOPS and up to 16,000 MiB/s throughput on the largest configurations.
- Practical trigger is earlier: when the working set no longer fits RAM, write-heavy traffic saturates CPU/WAL/disk IOPS, or maintenance (vacuum, index builds, backups) becomes operationally risky. Vanilla PostgreSQL has no built-in horizontal sharding — you add Citus, Vitess, or application-level sharding when vertical scaling stops helping.

MongoDB (replica set → sharded cluster):
- MongoDB guidance: plan sharding when a collection approaches ~2–3 TB, or when a large instance (e.g., Atlas M60 with 4 TB disk) sits at 60–70% CPU/RAM/storage utilization for sustained periods.
- High sustained write throughput on a single primary is another signal — the book's rule applies directly: one leader can become the bottleneck even if total cluster storage is fine.

Redis (in-memory, cluster sharding):
- Data must fit in RAM on the nodes serving it (unless using tiered/flash extensions). Set `maxmemory` to ~70–80% of host RAM — e.g., ~22–26 GB on a 32 GB host, ~45–51 GB on 64 GB, ~90–102 GB on 128 GB; AWS ElastiCache's largest single node (cache.r7g.16xlarge) offers up to 512 GiB.
- A single open-source Redis process is single-threaded for commands — typical throughput is on the order of tens of thousands to low hundreds of thousands of ops/sec depending on value size and pipelining; Redis Enterprise benchmarks show ~5M ops/sec per large node with many shards. Shard when one node's memory (often before a ~64–128 GB working set on OSS Redis) or command throughput is saturated.

DynamoDB (always partitioned internally):
- Every partition supports up to 3,000 read request units/sec and 1,000 write request units/sec (1 RCU = one strongly consistent 4 KB read; 1 WCU = one 1 KB write).
- On-demand tables also have a soft account/table throughput ceiling of 40,000 read and 40,000 write request units/sec per table (adjustable via Service Quotas). You redesign partition keys or write-shard hot keys when individual partitions hit the 3K/1K limits — not when the table as a whole is "full."

Cross-cutting book thresholds useful in design reviews:
- HBase auto-splits a region at ~10 GB by default, or when write throughput stays above a configured threshold.
- Fixed-shard designs (Citus, Riak, Elasticsearch) often pre-create hundreds or thousands of logical shards — e.g., 1,000 shards on 10 nodes (100 per node) — so node add/remove moves whole shards, not individual keys.

Comment: Measure your actual load parameters (data size, write RPS, working-set RAM ratio, p99) before sharding — the book's point is that sharding "adds complexity" and is "mostly relevant at large scale."

Sources: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/CHAP_Storage.html , https://www.mongodb.com/resources/products/capabilities/sharding , https://redis.io/docs/latest/operate/rs/databases/memory-performance/ , https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/bp-partition-key-design.html

theory.question: Pros and cons of using sharding for multitenant SaaS (one shard per tenant vs several tenants per shard)
theory.answer:
Multitenant sharding trades operational isolation and compliance convenience against overhead and cross-tenant feature cost.

Kleppmann describes SaaS tenants as separate datasets — e.g., each business in an email marketing product — and notes you can give each tenant its own shard or group several small tenants into one larger shard.

Pros:
- Resource isolation: "If one tenant performs a computationally expensive operation, it is less likely that other tenants' performance will be affected if they are running on different shards."
- Permission isolation: a bug in access control is "less likely that you will accidentally give one tenant access to another tenant's data if those tenants' datasets are stored physically separately."
- Cell-based architecture extends the idea to app services plus storage — faults stay inside one cell.
- Per-tenant backup/restore without touching other tenants.
- Regulatory compliance: GDPR/CCPA export and deletion become "simple data export and deletion operations on their shard" when personal data is shard-local.
- Data residence: assign a tenant's shard to a specific region for residency laws.
- Gradual schema rollout: migrate one tenant at a time, reducing blast radius.

Cons / challenges:
- Assumes each tenant fits on one node; a single huge tenant needs internal sharding on top of tenant sharding.
- Many small tenants → one shard each wastes overhead; grouping tenants means rebalancing as they grow.
- Cross-tenant features (analytics, search across customers, platform admin views) require joins or fan-out across shards — "these become harder to implement if you need to join data across multiple shards."
- Distributed transactions across shards are slow if a write must touch multiple tenants atomically.

Recommendation pattern: shard-by-tenant for large enterprise customers with strict isolation/residency needs; pool small tenants on shared shards with a tenant ID in every partition key; plan tenant migration tooling early.

Comment: AWS cell-based architecture whitepaper and Citus schema-based sharding are production implementations of this model.

theory.question: Key-range sharding: how it works, when to choose it, and how rebalancing happens
theory.answer:
Key-range sharding assigns each shard a contiguous interval of partition keys — like encyclopedia volumes sorted by title.

"One way of sharding is to assign a contiguous range of partition keys (from a minimum to a maximum) to each shard." Within a shard, keys are stored in sorted order (B-tree/SSTable), which makes range scans efficient: "they let you easily fetch, say, all the readings from a particular month."

Boundaries may be manual (Vitess) or automatic (Bigtable/HBase, MongoDB range sharding, CockroachDB, FoundationDB). Ranges are not necessarily equal width — volume 12 may cover T–Z — so boundaries adapt to keep shards roughly even.

Choose key-range when:
- You need range queries on the partition key (time-series, lexicographic browsing).
- You can structure the key so related reads cluster without creating write hot spots (prefix sensor ID before timestamp).

Main downside — hot shards: "if the key is a timestamp, then the shards correspond to ranges of time—for example, one shard per month. If you write data from the sensors to the database as the measurements happen, all the writes will end up going to the same shard." Fix by changing the leading key component, not by abandoning ranges entirely.

Rebalancing: split a shard into subranges when it grows too large or too hot; merge adjacent small shards after deletes. Splits are expensive — "requires all its data to be rewritten into new files" — and often hit an already-busy shard. HBase defaults to splitting at ~10 GB; hot shards may split on throughput even with little data.

Comment: Monotonic timestamp keys are a classic footgun — App Engine and HBase docs echo the same warning Kleppmann cites.

theory.question: Hash-modulo sharding (`hash(key) % N`): mechanism and why changing node count is painful
theory.answer:
Hash-modulo maps each key to `hash(key) % N` where N is the current node count.

After hashing the partition key, "your first thought may be to take the hash value modulo the number of nodes in the system (using the % operator in many programming languages). For example, hash(key) % 10 would return a number from 0 to 9."

This spreads skewed keys uniformly if the hash function is good (MongoDB MD5, Cassandra/ScyllaDB Murmur3). Avoid language default `hashCode()` / `Object#hash` — "the same key may have a different hash value in different processes, making them unsuitable for sharding."

Fatal flaw on cluster resize: "if the number of nodes N changes, most of the keys have to be moved from one node to another." Adding a fourth node to a three-node cluster reassigns roughly three-quarters of keys. "The mod N function is easy to compute, but it leads to very inefficient rebalancing because there is a lot of unnecessary movement of records from one node to another."

Use only when N is fixed forever (rare) or as a teaching baseline — production systems prefer fixed logical shards or consistent hashing instead.

Comment: Kleppmann's Java hashCode blog post remains the canonical warning for DIY sharding in JVM services.

theory.question: Fixed number of logical shards (many shards, fewer nodes): how it works and its limits
theory.answer:
Pre-partition into many logical shards, assign multiple shards per node, and move whole shards when nodes join or leave.

"One simple but widely used solution is to create many more shards than there are nodes and assign several shards to each node. For example, a database running on a cluster of 10 nodes may be split into 1,000 shards from the outset, so that 100 shards are assigned to each node. A key is then stored in shard number hash(key) % 1,000."

On node add/remove, reassign some shards to rebalance. "Only entire shards are moved between nodes, which is cheaper than splitting shards. The number of shards does not change, nor does the assignment of keys to shards. The only thing that changes is the assignment of shards to nodes." During transfer, the old mapping still serves traffic.

Pick a shard count divisible by many factors so you can scale to various node counts without powers of two. Assign more shards to beefier nodes to handle heterogeneous hardware.

Used by Citus, Riak, Elasticsearch, Couchbase.

Limits:
- You cannot have more nodes than shards.
- Wrong initial count forces "an expensive resharding operation" — split every shard, heavy disk use, sometimes downtime.
- Shard size tracks total data: too large → slow recovery/rebalance; too small → overhead. "The best performance is achieved when the size of shards is 'just right.'"

Comment: Elasticsearch's default number of primary shards is the same class of one-time decision — reindex required to change it.

theory.question: Hash-range sharding: combine hashing with dynamic range splits
theory.answer:
Hash-range sharding assigns each shard a contiguous range of hash values, not raw keys — getting even spread without fixing shard count upfront.

"If the required number of shards can't be predicted in advance, it's better to use a scheme in which the number of shards can adapt easily to the workload." Pure key-range risks hot spots on nearby keys; pure fixed-modulo risks rigid shard counts. Hash-range hashes first: "even if the input keys are very similar (e.g., consecutive timestamps), their hashes are uniformly distributed."

Example: 16-bit hash 0–65,535 split into four shards owning equal hash subranges. Split a shard when it grows too big or too loaded — expensive, but "the number of shards adapts to the volume of data rather than being fixed in advance."

Downside vs raw key-range: range queries on the partition key scatter across all shards. Exception: composite keys where only the first column is the partition key — range queries on later columns stay within one shard if the prefix is fixed.

Cassandra/ScyllaDB variant: split hash space into many ranges with random boundaries, ~16 ranges per node (Cassandra default) or 256 (ScyllaDB); on node add, transfer subranges so the newcomer gets a fair share "without transferring more data than necessary."

YugabyteDB, DynamoDB, and MongoDB offer hash-range style sharding.

Comment: This is the default mental model for DynamoDB — partition key hash determines the partition; sort key gives ordering inside the partition.

theory.question: Consistent hashing: properties and tradeoffs vs range splits
theory.answer:
Consistent hashing maps keys to shards so that adding or removing shards moves only a small fraction of keys.

"A consistent hashing algorithm is a hash function that maps keys to a specified number of shards in a way that satisfies two properties: The number of keys mapped to each shard is roughly equal. When the number of shards changes, as few keys as possible are moved from one shard to another."

"Consistent" here is unrelated to replication consistency or ACID — it means keys tend to stay on the same shard when possible.

Cassandra/ScyllaDB's ring is "similar to the original definition of consistent hashing," but alternatives exist: rendezvous (highest random weight) hashing and jump consistent hashing. Those assign individual keys from existing nodes to a newcomer rather than splitting existing shards into subranges — "which is preferable depends on the application."

Compare to hash-range splits: range-based systems move contiguous hash intervals; rendezvous/jump may scatter a new node's keys across the cluster. Range splits preserve locality for bulk transfers; scattered assignment can spread load faster but complicates data locality.

Consistent hashing alone does not fix hot keys — "ensures that keys are uniformly distributed across nodes, but that doesn't mean that the actual load is uniformly distributed."

Comment: Amazon's Dynamo paper popularized consistent hashing; production systems often combine it with virtual nodes (vnodes) for even distribution.

theory.question: Hot key / hot shard problem: causes and mitigation techniques
theory.answer:
A hot shard carries disproportionate load; a hot key drives that load on one partition.

"A shard with disproportionately high load is called a hot shard or hot spot. If one key has a particularly high load (e.g., a celebrity in a social network), we call it a hot key." Skew makes sharding ineffective — "all the load could end up on one shard, so 9 out of 10 nodes are idle."

Causes:
- Key-range sharding on timestamps → all current writes hit one time bucket.
- Hash sharding with skewed access (celebrity user ID, viral post ID) — even uniform key distribution does not imply uniform traffic.
- Secondary-index lookups concentrating on one value (all `color:red` cars).

Mitigations:
1. Fix the partition key design — prefix sensor ID before timestamp; avoid monotonic leading keys.
2. Infrastructure split — with range or hash-range sharding, "put an individual hot key in a shard by itself, perhaps even assigning it a dedicated machine."
3. Application-level write splitting — append random digits to a hot key so writes spread across N shards; reads must query all splits and merge. "Having split the writes across multiple keys, any reads now have to do additional work" and read volume per sub-key is not reduced.
4. Track which keys are artificially split; promote/demote as traffic changes (viral posts calm down after days).
5. Separate strategies for read-hot vs write-hot keys.
6. Managed heat management — DynamoDB adaptive capacity / heat management automatically rebalances or isolates hot partitions (details vendor-specific).

Automatic shard splits on throughput (HBase, etc.) help hot shards but split cost can "exacerbate" load on an already-busy shard.

Comment: DynamoDB Contributor Insights and Redis hot-key detection in cluster mode are the operational dashboards for this.

theory.question:
Request routing to shards: three approaches, coordination with ZooKeeper/etcd, and solutions to shared hard problems:
- Who decides shard→node mapping, and how is the coordinator fault-tolerant without split brain?
- How do routers learn mapping changes?
- During shard migration, requests may still be in flight to the old node — need cutover handling.
theory.answer:
Request routing answers: given a key, which IP:port owns its shard?

"If you want to read or write a particular key, how do you know which node—that is, which IP address and port number—you need to connect to?" Unlike stateless app servers, "a request for a key can be handled only by a node that is a replica for the shard containing that key."

Three approaches:
1. Contact any node (round-robin LB) — owner serves locally; otherwise forwards to the right node and relays the reply.
2. Dedicated routing tier (e.g., MongoDB `mongos`) — shard-aware load balancer that does not store data.
3. Shard-aware clients — connect directly to the correct node with no proxy.

Coordinator fault tolerance (who decides mapping, avoiding split brain):
- A small control plane holds the authoritative shard→node map — not individual data nodes and not DNS. Operators or an auto-rebalancer propose moves; only the coordinator commits them.
- The coordinator itself runs as a replicated quorum service (ZooKeeper ZAB, etcd Raft, MongoDB config-server replica set). Writes need a majority of replicas to ack, so a partitioned minority cannot publish a conflicting map — that is how split brain is avoided.
- Single-writer semantics: one elected leader applies mapping changes; followers replicate the log. Clients and routers read from any healthy replica but mutations go through the leader.
- Fencing with monotonic epochs: every mapping update carries a version/generation number (ZK zxid, etcd revision, MongoDB config version). Data nodes and routers reject or ignore assignments from a stale epoch — a zombie leader that lost quorum cannot overwrite a newer map.
- Data nodes register liveness (ephemeral ZK nodes, lease heartbeats). If a node is declared dead, the coordinator reassigns its shards only after the lease expires — avoids two nodes believing they own the same shard.
- Built-in Raft on data nodes (Kafka controller, YugabyteDB/TiDB placement driver, ScyllaDB) follows the same quorum rules without a separate ZK cluster.
- Riak gossip alternative: peers exchange mapping state without a central coordinator — simpler ops, weaker guarantees, possible divergent views tolerated because Riak already offers eventual consistency.

How routers learn mapping changes:
- Push, not poll-first: routing tier (`mongos`, Vitess VTGate, HBase client) and shard-aware drivers subscribe to the coordinator — ZooKeeper watches, etcd watch API, gRPC streams from a config service. On any mapping change, the coordinator notifies subscribers immediately.
- Local cache + version check: each router keeps an in-memory shard→node table and its epoch. On watch fire, fetch the new map atomically (single ZK znode or versioned config blob). Serve lookups from cache; compare epoch on every request or periodically — if local epoch < authoritative, block briefly and refresh.
- Stale-cache safety net: if a request lands on the wrong node (client missed an update), the owner returns a redirect or proxy (`MOVED`/`ASK` in Redis Cluster, MongoDB `movePrimary` errors) so correctness does not depend on perfect push delivery.
- Watch reconnect: on session loss, re-establish watch and do a full snapshot read before resuming — ZK and etcd guarantee you see every change since the last known revision.
- Polling fallback: when push is unavailable, poll the config server with exponential backoff and jitter; never rely on DNS TTL alone — "shard assignment changes faster and needs the coordination layer."
- Rollout ordering: publish the new map to the coordinator first; routers refresh before data nodes delete old copies (pairs with cutover below). Staggered refresh or version gates prevent a thundering herd of reconnects.

Cutover handling for in-flight requests during shard moves:
- Keep the old shard→node mapping authoritative while data copies — "the old assignment of shards is used for any reads and writes that happen while the transfer is in progress." Routers and clients must not flip to the new owner until the move is complete.
- Copy in rate-limited batches; track per-key or per-range migration state (`copying` → `ready` → `cutover`).
- Writes during copy: dual-write to source and destination, or forward writes arriving at the old node to the new one (MongoDB balancer, Vitess resharding). Reads: serve from source until caught up, then dual-read briefly or forward on cache miss.
- Atomic routing flip: once destination has a consistent snapshot plus all writes since copy started, publish the new mapping in one versioned update (ZK/etcd/config server). Stale clients still hitting the old node get redirected or proxied.
- Drain phase: old node rejects new ownership for moved keys but keeps serving in-flight requests until TTL/connection drain completes; only then delete local copies.
- Fixed-shard moves are simpler — whole shard is either on node A or B; key-range splits need finer-grained per-subrange state because one physical shard may own both old and new intervals mid-split.

Production examples: "Many distributed data systems rely on a separate coordination service such as ZooKeeper or etcd to keep track of shard assignments." HBase and SolrCloud use ZooKeeper; MongoDB uses config servers + `mongos`; Kubernetes etcd tracks service placement; Kafka/YugabyteDB/TiDB/ScyllaDB embed Raft instead. DNS finds node IPs but cannot track fast-moving shard ownership.

Comment: Vitess VTGate is another routing-tier example for MySQL sharding.

theory.question: Local vs global secondary indexes on a sharded database — write path, read path, and when to pick each
theory.answer:
Secondary indexes on sharded data do not map cleanly to shards; you either partition the index with the primary data (local) or shard the index separately (global).

The baseline assumption: "The sharding schemes we have discussed so far rely on the client knowing the partition key for any record it wants to access." Secondary indexes break that — they search by non-primary attributes (user 123's actions, `color = red`, full-text terms).

Local (document-partitioned) index:
- "Each shard independently maintains its own secondary indexes, covering only the records in that shard."
- Write: touch only the shard holding the record — cheap, no cross-shard index transaction.
- Read by secondary value without knowing partition key: "send the query to all shards and combine the results" — scatter-gather. Parallel scatter still suffers tail latency amplification; "adding more shards lets you store more data, but it doesn't increase your query throughput if every shard has to process every query anyway."
- Used by MongoDB, Riak, Cassandra, Elasticsearch, SolrCloud, VoltDB.

Global (term-partitioned) index:
- "A global index that covers data in all shards" but "can't just store that index on one node" — the index itself is sharded, often by indexed term/hash of term.
- Read a single term (e.g., `color = red`): hit one index shard for the postings list.
- Fetch actual rows: still read from every primary shard that owns those IDs.
- Multi-term AND (color + make): intersect postings lists — slow if lists are long and live on different index shards.
- Write: one record update may touch multiple index shards — "harder to keep the secondary index in sync." Options: distributed transaction or async index (DynamoDB global indexes — reads may be stale).

Pick local when writes dominate and secondary queries are rare or can tolerate scatter-gather. Pick global when read throughput on indexed lookups is high and postings lists stay short.

Comment: CockroachDB, TiDB, YugabyteDB use global indexes; DynamoDB supports both — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/GSI.html

theory.question: Automatic vs manual shard rebalancing — tradeoffs and when a human should stay in the loop
theory.answer:
Rebalancing question: do shard splits and moves happen automatically, manually, or hybrid (suggested plan, human commit)?

Fully automatic (DynamoDB, some key-range systems): convenient — "less operational work" and can autoscale within minutes. Risks: rebalancing "requires rerouting requests and moving a large amount of data" and can "overload the network or the nodes." Near max write throughput, splitting "might not even be able to keep up with the rate of incoming writes." Combined with auto failure detection, an overloaded slow node may be declared dead, triggering more moves — "cascading failure."

Manual / human-in-the-loop: slower but prevents surprises; useful for known traffic spikes ("Cyber Monday holiday sales or ticket sales for a popular athletic event such as the World Cup").

Hybrid: Couchbase and Riak auto-generate a proposed assignment; administrator commits before it takes effect.

Key-range splits are especially costly — rewrite entire shard files under load. Fixed-shard moves transfer whole shard datasets with clearer boundaries.

Operational checklist: monitor rebalance bandwidth, write lag during moves, dual-read/dual-write cutover for in-flight requests, and disable or dampen auto-failover during known rebalance events.

Comment: Kubernetes cluster autoscaler vs manual node pool sizing follows the same human-in-the-loop philosophy.

practice.task: [PostgreSQL] Choose a partition key for IoT sensor readings — key-range vs hash-range
practice.description:
An IoT platform stores temperature readings in PostgreSQL (you will later shard with Citus or an application router). Schema:

```
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
  sensor_id   BIGINT       NOT NULL,
  recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL,
  value_c     REAL         NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, recorded_at)
);
```

Workload:
- 50,000 sensors active concurrently
- Each sensor writes once per second (50,000 inserts/sec aggregate)
- Hot query A: all readings for sensor 42 between `2026-06-01` and `2026-06-07`
- Hot query B: all readings across all sensors for `2026-06-19` (daily rollup)

The table will grow to tens of terabytes. Compare partition keys:
1. `recorded_at` only (monthly key ranges)
2. `sensor_id` only (hash-range)
3. `(sensor_id, recorded_at)` with `sensor_id` as partition key

For each, state write distribution, whether query A and B stay efficient, and how you would rebalance.

solutions.solution:
Partition by `recorded_at` with monthly key-range shards.

Writes: every live sensor inserts into the current month shard — classic hot shard. Kleppmann: "if the key is a timestamp, then the shards correspond to ranges of time—for example, one shard per month. If you write data from the sensors to the database as the measurements happen, all the writes will end up going to the same shard (the one for this month), so that shard will be overloaded with writes while others sit idle."

Query A (one sensor, one week): efficient range scan in one shard if you know the month.

Query B (all sensors, one day): must scan the entire month's shard — huge read amplification.

Rebalancing: split the hot month shard under write pressure — "the cost of splitting can exacerbate that load."

Bad because 50k writes/sec on one shard defeats the purpose of sharding.

Partition by `sensor_id` using hash-range (hash sensor_id → shard).

Writes: 50k sensors hash across shards — even spread. Matches the book's fix: "prefix each timestamp with the sensor ID" so load spreads when many sensors are active.

Query A: point to one shard (known sensor_id), range scan on `recorded_at` inside that shard — efficient.

Query B: scatter-gather across all shards for the day — expensive but rare rollups can run on a warehouse replica or pre-aggregated table.

Rebalancing: hash-range splits when shards grow (Citus shard split or move). No mass key remapping on node add if you use many logical shards.

Good default for write-heavy ingestion with per-sensor reads. Limitation: cross-sensor time-window analytics need a separate pipeline.

Use `sensor_id` as partition key (hash-range), sort by `recorded_at` within shard — "you can still have efficient range queries among the records with the same partition key."

Citus setup sketch:
```
SELECT create_distributed_table('sensor_readings', 'sensor_id');
CREATE INDEX ON sensor_readings (sensor_id, recorded_at);
```

Writes: 50k/sec spread across N shards. Monitor shard size (~target under tens of GB per shard; HBase uses 10 GB default split threshold as a ballpark).

Query A:
```
SELECT * FROM sensor_readings
WHERE sensor_id = 42
  AND recorded_at BETWEEN '2026-06-01' AND '2026-06-07';
```
Single-shard index range scan.

Query B: do not serve from OLTP shards — stream readings to ClickHouse/BigQuery partitioned by day, or maintain daily rollup materialized view per shard merged at query time.

Hot sensor edge case: one faulty sensor spamming 10k/sec — detect outlier `sensor_id`, temporarily route to dedicated shard or rate-limit at ingest.

Migration: start single-node Postgres → enable Citus → `create_distributed_table` on empty table or use logical replication cutover. Keep rollups off the critical write path.

Rebalancing: Citus moves shard ranges between workers; run during low traffic with rebalance progress monitoring. Human approves rebalance plan if near capacity (hybrid ops model from the chapter).

Comment: This mirrors Kleppmann's sensor example — prefix sensor ID before timestamp to avoid time hot spots.

practice.task: [MongoDB] Local vs global secondary index for a sharded used-car marketplace
practice.description:
A used-car site shards listings by `listing_id` (hash sharding). Each document:

```
{ "_id": 742, "make": "Toyota", "color": "red", "price": 12000, "city": "Austin" }
```

`_id` 0–499 on shard 0, 500–999 on shard 1, etc.

Queries:
- Q1: get listing by `_id` (very frequent)
- Q2: all red cars under $15,000 (frequent browse filter)
- Q3: all Toyota listings in Austin (moderate)

Indexes considered:
- Local: each shard indexes only its listings (`color`, `make+city`)
- Global: `color` index sharded by color value; `make` index sharded by make

Pick index strategy, show which shards each query hits, and how a price drop update propagates.

solutions.solution:
Global secondary index on `color` only; no index on make/city.

Q2 (`color=red`, `price<15000`): one index shard for `red` postings list — fast index lookup. But fetching full documents still requires reads from every primary shard that owns matching IDs. If red cars are common, postings list is huge — "if they are long, it can be slow to send them over the network to compute their intersection" with price filter.

Q3: full collection scan on all shards — no index.

Write on price change: update primary shard + global color index shard (maybe multiple if color changes). Async global index → stale browse results.

Bad when Q3 matters and when red postings list grows without bound — global index write amplification for every new listing touches the color term's shard.

Local secondary indexes on each shard: `{ color: 1, price: 1 }` and `{ make: 1, city: 1 }`.

Q1: single shard by `_id` — optimal.

Q2: scatter-gather — `mongos` queries every shard with `{ color: 'red', price: { $lt: 15000 } }`, merge results. Write path stays simple: only the shard owning listing 742 updates its local index.

Q3: scatter-gather with `{ make: 'Toyota', city: 'Austin' }` on all shards.

Pros match the book: "Only a single shard needs to be updated on write." Cons: Q2/Q3 latency grows with shard count — tail latency amplification on parallel scatter.

Good when writes dominate and shard count is modest (<20). Limitation: browse QPS does not scale linearly with more shards.

Hybrid — local compound indexes for OLTP + search tier for browse.

MongoDB sharded collection:
```
sh.shardCollection('cars.listings', { _id: 'hashed' })
db.listings.createIndex({ color: 1, price: 1 })  // local
```

Q1: targeted by `_id`.

Q2 at scale: dual-write listings to Elasticsearch (or MongoDB Atlas Search) for `color` + `price` filters — search engine uses inverted indexes (global term space) while Mongo remains source of truth. Avoids scatter-gather tail latency on every browse.

If you must stay Mongo-only and Q2 is hot with many shards: consider global index on `color` only if postings lists stay short (narrow colors) and reads >> writes — "global indexes are useful if read throughput is higher than write throughput, and if the postings lists are not too long." Monitor index shard hot spots on popular colors.

Price drop update path:
1. Update document on primary shard (hash `_id`).
2. Local index updated on same shard atomically in one document write.
3. Async change stream → Elasticsearch update by `_id`.

For DynamoDB-style global index comparison: global secondary index would shard `color:red` on one index partition — watch for 1,000 WCU/partition limit equivalent (hot color term).

Migration: add local indexes online per shard; backfill search index via change streams; feature-flag browse to search tier.

Comment: Kleppmann's car color/make example maps directly to local vs global tradeoff in the chapter.

practice.task: [Coding] Implement consistent hashing — rebalance plan on node add and node remove
practice.description:
You operate a sharded key-value cache. Keys are opaque strings (session IDs, cache keys). Starting cluster: `node-a`, `node-b`, `node-c`. Two topology changes happen on the same 10,000-key dataset (`key-00000` … `key-09999`):
1. Add: `node-d` joins (3 → 4 nodes)
2. Remove: `node-d` leaves after a hardware failure (4 → 3 nodes; keys on `node-d` must be redistributed)

Implement consistent hashing on a ring with virtual nodes.

Write code that:
- Implements `get_node(key)` for lookups
- Prints how many keys change owner for add and for remove
- Returns an explicit migration list `{key, from_node, to_node}` for each change
- Implements `add_node(node)` and `remove_node(node)` that return the migration plan without requiring a full-cluster rescan in production (scan all keys here for the exercise)

Starter skeleton:

```python
import hashlib

KEYS = [f"key-{i:05d}" for i in range(10_000)]
NODES_3 = ["node-a", "node-b", "node-c"]
NODES_4 = NODES_3 + ["node-d"]

def stable_hash(key: str) -> int:
    return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
```

solutions.solution:
Full `HashRing` with `add_node` and `remove_node`, migration batches, and cutover notes.

```python
import hashlib
import bisect
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

KEYS = [f"key-{i:05d}" for i in range(10_000)]
VNODE_COUNT = 128
NODES_3 = ["node-a", "node-b", "node-c"]
NODES_4 = NODES_3 + ["node-d"]

def stable_hash(key: str) -> int:
    return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)

@dataclass
class HashRing:
    vnode_count: int = VNODE_COUNT
    _positions: list[int] = field(default_factory=list)
    _owners: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    _key_cache: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def _insert_vnodes(self, node: str) -> None:
        for i in range(self.vnode_count):
            pos = stable_hash(f"{node}#{i}")
            idx = bisect.bisect(self._positions, pos)
            self._positions.insert(idx, pos)
            self._owners.insert(idx, node)

    def _remove_vnodes(self, node: str) -> None:
        for i in reversed([i for i, o in enumerate(self._owners) if o == node]):
            del self._positions[i]
            del self._owners[i]

    def build(self, nodes: list[str]) -> None:
        self._positions.clear()
        self._owners.clear()
        self._key_cache.clear()
        for node in sorted(nodes):
            self._insert_vnodes(node)

    def get_node(self, key: str) -> str:
        if key in self._key_cache:
            return self._key_cache[key]
        if not self._positions:
            raise RuntimeError("ring is empty")
        idx = bisect.bisect(self._positions, stable_hash(key)) % len(self._positions)
        self._key_cache[key] = self._owners[idx]
        return self._key_cache[key]

    def _diff(self, keys: list[str], before: dict[str, str]) -> list[dict]:
        self._key_cache.clear()
        plan = []
        for k in keys:
            new_owner = self.get_node(k)
            if before[k] != new_owner:
                plan.append({"key": k, "from": before[k], "to": new_owner})
        return plan

    def add_node(self, node: str, keys: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
        before = {k: self.get_node(k) for k in keys}
        self._insert_vnodes(node)
        return self._diff(keys, before)

    def remove_node(self, node: str, keys: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
        before = {k: self.get_node(k) for k in keys}
        self._remove_vnodes(node)
        if not self._positions:
            raise RuntimeError("cannot remove last node")
        return self._diff(keys, before)

def print_batches(label: str, plan: list[dict]) -> None:
    batches: dict[tuple[str, str], int] = defaultdict(int)
    for m in plan:
        batches[(m["from"], m["to"])] += 1
    print(f"\n=== {label}: {len(plan)} keys ({100*len(plan)/len(KEYS):.1f}%) ===")
    for (src, dst), n in sorted(batches.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
        print(f"  {src} → {dst}: {n}")

# --- ADD: 3 → 4 ---
ring = HashRing()
ring.build(NODES_3)
add_plan = ring.add_node("node-d", KEYS)

print("=== rebalance cost: ADD 3 → 4 ===")
print(f"consistent hash: {len(add_plan):5d} keys")
print_batches("add migrations", add_plan)
assert all(m["to"] == "node-d" or m["from"] != "node-d" for m in add_plan)

# --- REMOVE: 4 → 3 (node-d failed) ---
ring2 = HashRing()
ring2.build(NODES_4)
rem_plan = ring2.remove_node("node-d", KEYS)

print("\n=== rebalance cost: REMOVE 4 → 3 (drop node-d) ===")
print(f"consistent hash: {len(rem_plan):5d} keys")
print_batches("remove migrations", rem_plan)

# On remove, only ex-node-d keys should move
assert all(m["from"] == "node-d" for m in rem_plan)
assert len(rem_plan) == len(add_plan)  # symmetric for same vnode layout
```

Sample output on 10k keys:
```
=== rebalance cost: ADD 3 → 4 ===
consistent hash:  2607 keys

=== add migrations: 2607 keys (26.1%) ===
  node-b → node-d: 1118
  node-c → node-d: 898
  node-a → node-d: 591

=== rebalance cost: REMOVE 4 → 3 (drop node-d) ===
consistent hash:  2607 keys

=== remove migrations: 2607 keys (26.1%) ===
  node-d → node-b: 1118
  node-d → node-a: 591
  node-d → node-c: 898
```

Key insight on remove: consistent hashing only reassigns keys whose clockwise successor vnode belonged to the removed node. Keys on a/b/c never move — you copy only the failed node's ~26% share.

Why this matches the chapter: "when the number of shards changes, as few keys as possible are moved from one shard to another." Virtual nodes (128 per physical node) smooth ownership; removing a node deletes its vnodes and keys fall to the next vnode on the ring.

Operational cutover (book: "old assignment of shards is used for any reads and writes that happen while the transfer is in progress"):

Add: publish new ring → dual-read → copy batches `(from, to)` with rate limits → delete from source after ack.

Remove: mark node-d down → generate `rem_plan` (only `from == node-d`) → survivors absorb keys → do not delete from node-d until copies verified (it may come back). If node-d is truly dead, plan is exactly the keys it owned.

Invalidate `_key_cache` on every ring mutation. Human approves rebalance near max throughput — auto-migration during overload risks cascading failure.

Extension: jump consistent hashing and rendezvous hashing also minimize moves on add/remove without a sorted ring.

Comment: Cassandra token removal and Dynamo vnode decommission follow this same migration-plan pattern.

practice.task: [Design] Mitigate a hot key on a sharded social feed (celebrity post)
practice.description:
A social app shards by `post_id` (hash-range). A celebrity posts; `post_id = 9001` receives:
- 200,000 comment writes/sec for 48 hours
- 2M reads/sec on comment count and top comments

One shard melts; other shards are idle. The partition key cannot change retroactively.

Design mitigations at application and infrastructure layers. Address write skew, read skew, and post-viral cooldown.

solutions.solution:
Scale up the physical machine holding the hot shard (vertical scaling only).

Does not fix hash-range hot key — all traffic still routes to one shard leader. "All the load could end up on one shard, so 9 out of 10 nodes are idle, and your bottleneck is the single busy node."

Adding replicas helps reads only if the system supports leaderless or read replicas on that shard; 200k writes/sec still hit one leader. DynamoDB analogy: single partition caps at 1,000 WCU/sec — scale-up does not apply.

Bad — ignores skew root cause.

Application-level write sharding for comments on known hot posts.

Kleppmann: "add a random number to the beginning or end of the key. Adding just two random digits would split the writes evenly across 100 keys."

Store comments under keys `9001:00` … `9001:99` (suffix 00–99). Writes spread across up to 100 hash partitions.

Reads: fetch comment count by querying all 100 suffixes and summing; top-comments query merges 100 partial lists (or maintain an async counter in Redis).

Track hot posts in a registry; apply suffix only to posts exceeding a write threshold.

Downside explicitly acknowledged: "The volume of reads to each shard of the hot key is not reduced; only the write load is split." 2M reads/sec still hurt — pair with a cached counter (Redis `GET`) and CDN for static top-comment snapshots.

Good stopgap without resharding the whole table.

Layered mitigation with lifecycle management.

1. Detection: monitor per-shard write QPS and per-key rates; auto-flag posts exceeding e.g. 5k writes/sec (DynamoDB heat management / Contributor Insights equivalent).

2. Write path (viral phase):
- Split comment writes with 2-digit suffix → 100 logical keys (book technique).
- Alternatively dedicate a single shard/range to `post_id=9001` if using hash-range ops that can isolate one key — "put an individual hot key in a shard by itself, perhaps even assigning it a dedicated machine."
- Buffer writes through Kafka partition keyed by suffix; consumers batch-insert to DB.

3. Read path:
- Maintain `comment_count` in Redis with `INCR` — serves 2M reads/sec without hitting DB.
- Top comments: precompute every N seconds; store in cache.
- Accept eventual consistency on counts (matches async fan-out patterns from replication chapter).

4. Cooldown: after 48h traffic drops, merge suffix buckets in background job; remove post from hot registry; collapse back to single `post_id` key for long-term storage.

5. Prevention for future posts: use time-bucketed or random prefix in partition key for high-follower accounts at post creation time (cannot change key later without migration).

6. Rebalancing caution: do not trigger automatic shard split on overloaded node during viral event without human approval — risk of "cascading failure" if autoscaler misreads overload as node death.

Tradeoffs documented: suffix splitting increases read complexity and bookkeeping — "you also need some way of keeping track of which keys are being split."
